content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 16, 2009


Mary Yen
Assistant General Counsel
Office of the General Counsel
State Bar of California
180 Howard Street
San Francisco, CA 94105

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-08-210

Dear Ms. Yen:

      This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Randall Difuntorum,¹
Director of Professional Competence of the State Bar of California, regarding the gift and
honoraria provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")² We base our advice solely
on the provisions of the Act and on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

      May Director Difuntorum accept gifts and honoraria without restriction from
sources that, according to the State Bar of California's conflict of interest code, the
director is not required to report on his statement of economic interest?


________________
¹ You provided the director's name on December 26, 2008 and stated that he had authorized the
advice request.
² The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-210
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

The Act does not restrict the director from accepting gifts and honoraria from
persons other than those whose disclosure is required under the agency's conflict of
interest code.

FACTS

Randall Difuntorum is the Director of Professional Competence of the State Bar
of California (the "State Bar") and a designated employee. The director's disclosure
categories under the State Bar's conflict of interest code are as follows:

• Vendors of typesetting, printing or duplicating services or equipment.
• Vendors of meeting space, food services and entertainment.
• Vendors of computers, computer hardware/maintenance/software, data
processing, web hosting/design, computer consulting services.
• Court reporting services.
• Vendors of office supplies/equipment/furniture.
• Travel agencies, hotels, meeting planning services, airlines, car rental agencies;
• Mass mailing services.
• Providers of continuing legal education, legal publications, online legal research
for California attorneys.

The director received an invitation to speak at an international conference in
Tokyo on March 7, 2009. The host for the conference is Hitotsubashi School of Law (the
"law school"). The law school is a public institution mainly funded by the national
government. The law school, jointly with the Japan Law Foundation and with financial
support from the Ministry of Education, is developing a proposed new system of
continuing legal education in professional ethics. The law school received a special
subsidy from the Ministry of Education for the project which is named "Development of
Continuing Legal Ethics Education."

The director has been invited to speak on California's system of continuing legal
education of professional ethics and the function of the State Bar in the system, and to
participate in a discussion. He will write a paper for the presentation at the conference
and which will be published at a later date. The host law school has offered to pay the
following:

• Travel, lodging and meals for three days,
• An honorarium of approximately 100,000 yen (approximately $1,105), and
• A payment for the paper of 5,000 yen (or approximately $55) per page.

File No. A-08-210
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 requires every state agency to adopt a conflict of interest code
designating those positions within the agency which involve the making of governmental
decisions. Employees of the agency who hold those positions must file statements of
economic interests. The code must also establish categories for disclosure which are
tailored to the types of decisions made by each designated position. (Section 87302.)

Effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series of statutes that restrict
the receipt of gifts and honoraria by public officials. They provide:

"No member of a state board or commission and no designated
employee of a state or local government agency shall accept an
honorarium from any source if the member or employee would be required
to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests"

Section 89502(c),
emphasis added.

"No member of a state board or commission or designated
employee of a state or local government agency shall accept gifts from any
single source in any calendar year with a total value of more than two
hundred fifty ($250)[1] if the member or employee would be required to
report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests."

Section 89503(c),
emphasis added.

Consequently, according to Section 89502 and Section 89503, the honoraria and
gift restrictions only apply to payments from disclosable sources.

The director has been invited to speak at an international conference in Tokyo on
March 7, 2009. The host for the conference is Hitotsubashi School of Law. The law
school is carrying out a project to propose a new system of continuing legal education on
professional ethics that is combined with education in law schools. This is a joint project
of the law school and the Japan Law Foundation with financial support from the Ministry
of Education. The director is not required to report any of the sources on his statement of
economic interests pursuant to his disclosure category.

_______________

[1] The gift limit is adjusted each odd-numbered year to reflect changes in the Consumer Price
Index. (Section 89503(f).) For the years 2009-2010, the gift limit is $420.

File No. A-08-210
Page No. 4

You ask whether the disclosure and honoraria prohibition apply to sources that are not required to be reported under the director's disclosure category. Such payments are not prohibited or reportable.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JW:jgl
